reasoning: A reasonable person would agree that rescuing a trapped, distressed pet facing freezing conditions takes precedence over the minor property damage of breaking a padlock, especially when the owner is unreachable and authorities will not intervene. The urgency and lack of alternatives make the exception obvious.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that preventing a Type 1 diabetic from carrying emergency glucose supplies is dangerous and unjust. Given the prior refusal by security to recognize this medical necessity, hiding the supplies is an obviously warranted exception to the 'no outside food' rule to prevent a medical emergency.

The user faces an immediate safety threat (approaching thunderstorm on an exposed ridge) with no safe public alternative due to the mudslide. A reasonable person would immediately agree that preserving human safety outweighs the minor infringement of walking across a private pasture, making this a clear case of necessity.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that saving a living creature from freezing to death takes precedence over a vacation rental's no-pets policy. The immediate, irreversible harm to the animal outweighs the abstract breach of contract or potential minor property risk.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that a rule banning electronics should not prevent a visually impaired person from using a digital magnifier to see art. Enforcing the ban against a disability aid is obviously unjust and violates the spirit of the rule (preventing recording), making the exception clearly warranted.
